speaker: interviewer
text: Leopold Page. This is roll two with Leopold Page.

speaker: interviewee
text: Pfefferberg.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we just arrived to Cracow.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we are not in Cracow yet. You see, in the city of [INAUDIBLE], because I was assistant to the doctor who was the chief operating doctor-- I was participating in all the operations-- not only on the Polish soliders, but also on the German soldiers who was brought to the hospital. We received a piece of paper signed by the army with a German spread eagle, you know, that we have a right to go outside the hospital to the field to pick up the wounded soldiers.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I kept this. You see? When we-- when they decide to move out from city of [INAUDIBLE], the German, and give it to the Russian so the river [INAUDIBLE] became the [INAUDIBLE] line, you know, between Russia and Germany from 1939 until later on, till June 1947-- till 1941. You know. They took us by train back to Germany. And the train stop in city of Cracow. And we-- they were holding us in the--

speaker: interviewee
text: And I know the city where, where I was born. I know the station, every inch of it. They put us in the first class waiting room. There were a couple hundred of the officers. And we were waiting because they were changing the train to take us to Germany. Then I noticed a strange fellow staying on the rear door holding a guard, a soldier, not SS man, not Gestapo-- soldier. I came to him because I spoke very well German, you know, educated language, German, not the slang. And I ask him a simple question. [BACKROUND NOISE] Can you [GERMAN] So I took the piece of paper what I got it from the authorities in [INAUDIBLE] and show it to him. Read it. He looking, and there it is written that I have a right to get out, to bring the-- so he opened the door. And he knew we are officer. Salute. Goodbye. I went out 6 o'clock in the morning. I jump on trolley car. And my, my home was exactly about five minutes by trolley car from there. I came home. About 6:15 I was home.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then I burned my officer uniform except the preachers what I dyed black so I could use them. Because I have beautiful boots, and were very good because it was October, November, December. And it was good to have it because in this time, all the Polish people was having high boots. You know? And I want to look pretty good like the general public. Who you find home? Who was--

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother was there. My father was there. And my sister was there. They were the closest. All my family was intact in this time. This was in beginning of October 1939.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then, when--

speaker: interviewee
text: Then I found out three days later from the lady who was in-- supervisor of the apartment building where we live-- and she likes me very much, because as a single man, I was sometimes coming after 10 o'clock when the door to the building was closed and you have to ring her up. You give her a tip. I give her a 20, 30 zlotys-- are like $5-$6 a month. And I have a key. And she likes me. Always I found her a gift or something. She's calling me and saying, Poldie, you have to hide. The Gestapo is looking for you. They were here, and they asked me to report the minute you come over.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I start to hide different places every night. I never come back home. Sometime I sneak home during the day to see my parents or my mother. My father was working in this particular time because they took all the businesses away with Jewish [INAUDIBLE] as one of the work at there-- volunteer. Not volunteer. They get paid a little bit, but not too much. He didn't make too much money.

speaker: interviewee
text: My money was-- my mother was the biggest supporter of--

speaker: interviewer
text: And you're supposed to, to wear the signs already?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. They came in, in-- about in October time with the order that every Jew over 13 years of age has to carry the armband. Now, I carried the armband when I considered it's important to carry. Mostly I keep it in the pocket, or I didn't wear it when I was out. I have a--

speaker: interviewer
text: Your grandfather and grandmother they were--

speaker: interviewee
text: My grandfather then was not alive. My grandmother was alive from- from my father's side. From my mother's side, both parents were dead already. But the family was intact. This means that his brother and sister and a cousin and uncle, family-- close to 100 members of the family was a tremendous-- from both sides-- was a tremendous family.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, so when you was working on this time something?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was working till the gymnasium was closed. This means till the 1st of January, 1940. Then they closed the school. So then you have to do something.

speaker: interviewer
text: So because I was very well known between the Jewish circle as a teacher, and my students' parents-- some of them were very wealthy-- when I-- for example-- I give a small example. One of my student's father has an import of the velour hats. In Poland, velour hats were expensive, like about $5 in this time. Like 35 zloty or 40 zloty, which was quite a bit of of money at this time.

speaker: interviewee
text: And somebody wanted, I got a few of those velour hats from him, and make a couple zloty or a few dollars. Then everything was permittable, except you cannot sell currency, like dollars, or German marks. This was-- or- or English pound. This was not allowed by the law and was punishable by death for the Jew or for Polacks maybe to go and-- and-- to jail for a few years or maybe even to a concentration camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me, how you met your wife?

speaker: interviewee
text: How, how I make my life?

speaker: interviewer
text: No, no. Your wife.

speaker: interviewee
text: I met my wife-- this is a-- she should probably told you, too. This was extraordinary story, because in this time I was living with my friends, not with my parents. I-

speaker: interviewer
text: So you left your parents.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And my friends still have the store wholesale skin-- raw skin, like for shoes, for clothes, for handbags, you understand. So we were trying to unload it as much as possible of the skins so that we will not be confiscated by the Germans because they were confiscating it. Again, this way I was making certain amount of profit and making living supporting my family. My father didn't make any money. My mother, mother still was interior decorator and still was making some money. But was very, very difficult, because we were kicked out from this apartment, I think. So by the end of October or beginning of September, we lost everything. When we received the apartment back, it was completely empty walls. Nothing in it. Everything disappeared. So we lost almost everything in about, I would say, half an hour time. Now, what happened with my wife-- this is a very interesting story. One day there came an order that the Jewish family has to accept people, for-- not only Jewish, they were liquidating the intelligentsia of the city of Lodz. They took about a thousand most prominent Jewish and non-Jewish and Gentile family out, confiscating everything in. Doctors, attorney, professors, and teachers. Mother of my-- my mother-in-law, mother of Mila, was a very prominent doctor. She was skin and venereology doctor.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she was first on the list. Mila didn't have to go with her. But she was only in this time 18 years, 19 years old, So you understand? she didn't want to stay alone. And she went with brother, and they sent her to city of Cracow, because in this time, we are also working to have a document that we are working. You see? We were also involved in the work in the Jewish Judenrat. I was assistant to the president. What was my job? I was keeping the appointment. [BACKROUND NOISE] I stay by the door. Everybody wants to see the president. And I was the man who was responsible, who can go and talk to the president.

speaker: interviewee
text: My-- my friend, what I stayed with him, was in charge [BACKROUND NOISE] in the welfare department there. So he got an order to go and pick up some family and assign some apartments for them. Look, he saw a young woman who was introduced as Dr. Maria Lewinson, and a beautiful young lady around 19, 20 years old, you understand. He said, we will-- we have-- he has three apartments to his disposal. He decides to take this Dr. Lewinson and her daughter, Ludmila Lewinson, to our apartment, because we have a tremendous apartment. And it was Friday evening. It was 6 o'clock in the evening. [LAUGHS] I tell you something.

speaker: interviewer
text: They came up in a droshka. You know what is a droshka? Horse and, and carriage. And was pouring. And I was waiting. I didn't know that he is bringing any company. And he said, Paul, I have a guest today, and you have to run and buy anything. The best you can buy. Ham, cheese, bread, liquor.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anything you can put hands on it, go and bring it home, because we have an important guest. And he introduced me to Dr. Lewinson and to Ludmila. And he said to-- [LAUGHS] --I have to introduce you to my best friend, Professor-- not professor, but-- and I met my wife.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then you--

speaker: interviewee
text: I met this way my wife [LAUGHS] Friday night.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long was-- until you married?

speaker: interviewee
text: I tell you something that's very interesting. He said to me, Pol, I will marry this girl. So stay away. So I stay away. But not long ago, later on in December I kissed her, and he said, you can have her. So I took it. And I married her.

speaker: interviewer
text: In a church?

speaker: interviewee
text: We got engaged on the day when my sister got married. That was in March when I was-- in this time, I was-- my 27 years birthday. And she was not even 20. One week after we got married, she was 20 years of age.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were married in a, in a, in a synagogue or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. We were married in a private synagogue. The synagogue was closed. In a private thing. And I came about 45 minutes later. [LAUGHS] I still was not sure or I'm doing the right thing. But we got married, and then we went home. And when we were using the trolley car, there were some young punks, you know, over there, the Nazis. And they kick us out and said, this, this Yiddish [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: What does that mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: Do your shit and get out [LAUGHS] of here.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in this time it was a very difficult already to live in Cracow, right?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, still was quite pleasant. And I was making pretty good money because I met Schindler, Oskar Schindler, who later on I found out that he's a very decent human being who was interpreter. And he came to my mother, commanded by a Jew, for my mother redecorate the apartment for the Jewish fellow, and he purchased from the Jewish fellow. He paid him 50,000 zloty, which was quite a big amount of money at this time. Maybe $5,000, $6,000. This Oskar Schindler came over and was recommended. My mother hesitated. And it's interesting, you know. I still have my pistol, you know. When I was dressing up, there was not the real military revolver. This I lost over there. It's buried in the ground in [INAUDIBLE] over there in the hospital someplace. I dig it in the ground. My little one, [INAUDIBLE], you know, when I was dating a girl and I went in the uniform, I put the pistol to impress her a little bit more. [LAUGHS] That's all  what was the fun for it. And when I saw-- when somebody was knocking on the door-- and I was alone with my mother-- and I-- my mother looked through the look area, you know, in the door, you know, the viewer. And she said, Pol, it's Gestapo. She saw through this door looker a guy with a big swastika, swastika. And he-- she was thinking he's Gestapo. So I have the pistol put behind my belt. I figure out probably I have to fire. That was stupid thinking. But this is way you think when you're young. And we still didn't know what kind problems you can have. And I said to her, let him in. And I hide behind the door and listen to the conversation. And from his tone of voice and his way how he was talking to my mother, I realized the man is some different type of guys, and I stepped forward.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he's taller-- he was taller than I, but I consider I was pretty handsome. And he was extremely handsome man. I look at his eye. He looks in my eyes, and what happened? Some thread of sympathy, some understanding between the German Catholic and the Jewish Polish officer started.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was always dressed very well, so after when he was talking with my mother and explain her what he want from her, and she still hesitated. She was afraid. So I told my mother, [NON-ENGLISH], go ahead and do it with him the business, because we need the money.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he said, you know, I see you have a wonderful shirt, pure silk shirt. And I need something like this. So I said to him in German-- everything's going in German-- do you know how much cost this? You know, I got fresh. Do you know how much costs this type of shirt?

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, I don't care how much costs this shirt, I would like to have a dozen. I said, it will cost you $5 apiece. I can be [INAUDIBLE] in zloty, you know. He took the money and he give me the money and said, take the measurement, get me this. And this way this started the friendship between Oskar Schindler and Leopold Pfefferberg what lasted till day he die in 1974 in October the 9th. [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewer
text: And then when they opened the-- when open-- when was the Cracow ghetto opened? What's happened with you then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now that-- we were living in this Cracow till-- in the city of Cracow until the end of 1940. Then the rumors came in that they were having a close part of city only for Jews to keep them under the supervision and protected them from the Polish population. That's the reason they want to consolidate them in one part of city in city of Cracow, down in [INAUDIBLE], there's a suburb. I personally didn't like the idea, but we said, let-- let's try to get the permission to, to do this. So we apply. Coincidentally, my family was not protected by the Jewish Federation because they were not essential worker over there, and we didn't get it. And we have to move from the city to any place we want east from Cracow.

speaker: interviewer
text: So because my brother-in-law, husband of my sister, Pauline, was from Sanok, they decide to go to Sanok. And I decide with Mila to go to Sanok, too. But then I got a bright idea, why should I move from Cracow? I was born here. I didn't do anything wrong. I will go to the city hall and ask them for the permission to stay in Cracow. So I went dressed beautifully with a Jewish armband. And I went there and asked for the mayor of the city, Mr. Pavlo. They said Mr. Pavlo is not here, but his assistant is here. I said, I don't care. I will talk to his assistant. And what they bringing me to a-- to a beautiful office, and behind the desk is an SS officer sitting, a handsome guy. And I couldn't believe it, my eye.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a friend of mine, who was-- from Vienna, who was instructor of Polish National Ski, Ski Team. In 1939 in February was first federation international ski exercises in Zakopane in the mountains-- winter type of Olympics game. And he was the instructor of Polish group. And they need somebody who is also instructor who speaks German. And the Polish National organi-- Ski Organization selected me. And I met-- we became a very good friend. He was coming to my home. We were going dancing. I introduced him to some lovely Jewish girls, you know. And everything went--

speaker: interviewer
text: When I look at him, I said I couldn't believe it. And he couldn't believe it. And he said to me, Pol, [NON-ENGLISH], what are you doing here? I said, I didn't have any permission to stay. You know I'm Jewish. He didn't know that I am Jewish in this time because I spoke German, but I look like another nice Polish fellow, you know. And did you do something wrong? And I said, no, I didn't do anything wrong. I was officer in Polish army, and I fight for Poland. And you are officer in German Army. You were fighting for German-- Germany. When this is a crime, this is the only crime I committed. So what would you like me to do? I said, I would like to have permission for my-- me, my wife, and my family to stay here in Cracow and go to the ghetto. We did-- they didn't call it the ghetto. They called this only a special section for protection of the Jewish population of city of Cracow against the Polish people. He said, OK. I will try to do it. I'll let you know. When you have to leave? I said, Sunday afternoon we're leaving. At this moment, I went home. And I said to my family, we have a chance to stay. Friday-- Sunday, we went over there to the assembly place where for which we were going to the city of Sanok. I didn't see this Mr. Br-- what is-- let me-- can you-- Give me this. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, and then?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't see anybody. This guy didn't show-- the [INAUDIBLE] doesn't showing up. But about 3 o'clock afternoon, somebody is yelling, Pfefferberg, Polie Pfefferberg. So I came over and say, you, you and your wife, you can get out. Go back to your apartment. But your parents I could not [INAUDIBLE]. That was the last I saw my parents and my sister, my brother-in-law, and my mother and father. Mila and I will stay home till March the-- 1941, when they decide to move all the Jewish population from the city to the ghetto. So we took little bit of our things because the furniture was impossible to-- expensive furniture. Expensive furniture. Mother pay about-- before the war in Vienna-- order about $3,000 for the furniture. Was, it was magnificent. [INTERPOSING VOICES] And we go to the ghetto. And we stay in the ghetto from March the-- from my birthday, March the 20th, till- till March the 20th, 1943.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's happened with your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm sorry?

speaker: interviewer
text: Your mother, father, and sister they went to?

speaker: interviewee
text: They went to Sanok. They stay in Sanok. What I got information after, that my sister, my brother-in-law, his brother, and his wife went under false paper. They got themselves false paper. I advised them to go across the River San-- because Sanok is on the River San-- to cross the River San and get to-- to Lvov and to go to Russia. But I found out later on-- and this was in 1942-- that my brother-in-law and my sister and his brother and his wife are in Czestochowa, and they are blackmailed. They need few thousand zloty. So I send them the few thousand zloty. That was the last time I heard from them. Then after the war, I found out that they were caught by coincidence. [BACKROUND NOISE] His brother with his wife survived, and they coincidentally got in the trap, you know. [INTERPOSING VOICES] They got apartment in Warsaw. And this apartment was under surveillance.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother and father?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were in city of Sanok. And they lost their life when the city of Sanok was liquidated. Probably they were-- they were sent to Grodno camp, or to Belzec, you know. I don't know what they did.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me. And then you--

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister was arrested with my brother-in-law in Warsaw in 1943. And they were executed in the forest of Palmiry, about 20 kilometer from Warsaw. I went there. I have some photos from the Jewish tombs and Catholic crosses, but no names. So I am not sure where they really were buried.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you came to, to the ghetto [BACKROUND NOISE] with Mila in 1941, from 1941 to 1943-- two years-- what was-- what happened in the ghetto? How you lived in the ghetto? What were you doing?

speaker: interviewee
text: You see, I, I, I, I was very-- how will I tell you? You know, when you have a connection-- and, remember, as a teacher I had lots of people I knew. And I was respected, you know, by the parents. When somebody has any chance to influence, like he can give me in Jewish [INAUDIBLE], what was run by the Jewish [INAUDIBLE], permission to get out. So I got the permission. It maybe cost me a few zloty that I could go outside. Going outside, I was riding on the bicycle. Always on the bicycle because that was my-- like a shield in protection. Because normally the Jew couldn't ride, ride the trolley car, he couldn't use the droshka. He could-- he could only walk and have the armband. The minute I was outside the ghetto, the armband went sticking out someplace and I was riding on the bicycle. And I have a lot of Polish friends-- tremendous amount of friends-- from the university, from the army, from the school, and, and they were very supporting. Very supporting. And everything I need something-- so I was smuggling some things like, for example, when somebody need [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] strawberry jam or, or, or juice to make wine. Was a winery in ghetto, because sugar, they couldn't get it. So I brought him about 50 bottles of it, because I knew somebody who was on the guard with the things and figured how else you could bring them in. You make a living. I was always traveling with a big bag. And in the bag you could find anything you want.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that was very dangerous.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, when I-- when I will be caught, I wouldn't be talking to you. [LAUGHS] I didn't think about the danger in this time. I was thinking that I'm so smart that I can avoid everything. But you never know what in the--

speaker: interviewer
text: You were sure that you would survive this ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm sorry?

speaker: interviewer
text: You were sure you would survive this ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was sure that I will survive the ghetto. [INTERPOSING VOICES] No question about it. That I will survive the war, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: You saw the, the people disappearing from the ghetto. Start to be--

speaker: interviewee
text: I tell you something, what it was. I have a chance to go out anytime. No problem. But Mila was a little bit scared. Remember, she was only 20, 21 years old. She never live yet. She didn't [INAUDIBLE]. Nothing. I was at least seven years older, and I saw something in my life already.

speaker: interviewer
text: But, but, you, you saw the people disappearing. You know that they are killed?

speaker: interviewee
text: I know they were going under false paper. I knew they were going to the border to Czechoslovakia, to Hungary. I knew everything. But, remember, Mila got one big complex. She was thinking that she looks too Jewish to go out. And I just tried to persuade her that she doesn't look Jewish. She looks like a French girl, like an Italian girl, like a Romanian girl. And she has a birth certificate that she was born in Romania, because she was born Romania. When she was not even--

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm not-- Yeah, I know. But I'm not asking this. I'm asking you did know in this time about the Jews are killed in Treblinka, in Belzec.

speaker: interviewee
text: I tell you-- I'll tell you what it is. In 19-- my uncle was a big shot in Jewish [INAUDIBLE]. He was arrested with the president of the [INAUDIBLE], because they were bribing a German official to allow the Jews to stay in Cracow not to get to ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is [INAUDIBLE]? [INAUDIBLE]?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's a Jewish federation. You know, Jewish council. So they arrested three people. One of them was the president, who-- the president was a teacher before the war. He was my teacher. When I became a teacher, I was teaching at the same high school what he was teaching. We were colleagues. This reason, I was selected by him to be-- give me the documentation that I work in the Jewish council. My uncle was a, a, a, a treasurer. And they tried to bribe a higher official to allow the Jews to stay as long as possible in their homes. And they were sending them every day to work, the forced labor. Because every day they assigned lots of people to work.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now when-- for example, when I was supposed to go this day to work, I took 10 zloty or 20 zloty, I give a guy, and he took my cart. He went for me there. You understand? So I didn't have to go. You could buy your time.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this way I have a chance to go and make some-- somebody has a diamond ring. A jeweler. He couldn't go out. He was locked out. He give me the diamond ring. I went out, outside. I sell it. I give him the amount he wanted, and the difference I kept as a commission. And this way I was making a living.

speaker: interviewer
text: But I'm asking you heard about the, the Jews are killed this time around Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: I tell you what happened. We knew that the Jews are rounded up and sent to the different places. Supposed-- supposed to go to a-- labor camps. Still one never dream that any murder will be committed in this particular time. Nobody dream in 1940, 1939, 1940, or '41 even.

speaker: interviewer
text: But when you-- when you-- first time--

speaker: interviewee
text: The first time I found out that my uncle was arrested in 1940 for the supposed to bribing this official who denounce him, he was sent to Auschwitz. And we were getting a note till one day came a little box with ashes and a note that my uncle, Joachim Goldfluss, died from [NON-ENGLISH], from the inflammation of the lung.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was in '41?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in '41. In this time, we already were getting information. For example, [INAUDIBLE] Polish officer who report themselves because the order came in that all reserve officer or any officer who is civilian now has to report themself to Gestapo.

speaker: interviewer
text: I didn't go because the Gestapo was looking for me. So I didn't register myself. But a few of my friends-- Polish friends-- who were also officers registered. And I told them not to do it. I don't like this. This is a trick. You see? They find out themselves in Auschwitz, and they will perish. Also my friend the priest. And in '42?

speaker: interviewee
text: In '41 already.

speaker: interviewer
text: But let's-- in '42, you, you knew already.

speaker: interviewee
text: '42 was different. We had already found out because one of the men from our camp was sent to Treblinka. He survive by hiding and then escaping. And the story is written that-- and his name was Buchner. And he came back to ghetto he was in his thirties. Young fellow, maybe a couple of years older. I knew him from the university. He was dark hair. When he come back, he was all white. And--
